content.text:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The conversation is summarized below:
Analysis:
Let me chronologically analyze the conversation:

1. **Initial Request**: User asked to list all files in `./docs` recursively. I attempted to use Bash but the result was cleared.

2. **Second Major Request**: User asked me to read all documentation files to understand the project completely. I read multiple files:
   - `docs/@start-here/` folder (RFQ, proposal_and_sow, README, email, VALIDATION-REPORT-TEMPLATE)
   - `docs/internal/` folder (README, CLIENT-CONTEXT, COMMERCIAL, DEMO-STRATEGY, etc.)
   - `docs/knowledge-base/` (INDEX, problem-definition, terminology-glossary, system-overview, ruc-system)
   - `docs/README.md`, `docs/RESEARCH_MAINTENANCE_SCHEDULING.md`, `docs/TECHNICAL_DEBT.md`

3. **Critical User Feedback**: User provided important corrections:
   - Database persistent is REQUIRED for demo (not optional)
   - Excel export is "nice to have" (not required)
   - Most documentation was AI-generated and caused SoW confusion
   - Project is at risk of being lost
   - Only RFQ and SoW are reliable (RFQ is source of truth from client)
   - Everything marked as "done" must be verified, nothing assumed working

4. **Document Updates**: I updated several documents to reflect critical status:
   - `CLAUDE.md` - Added critical warnings, marked everything as unverified
   - `docs/TECHNICAL_DEBT.md` - Updated priorities, added warning about AI-generated content
   - `docs/internal/README.md` - Added trust level warnings

5. **Technology Stack Verification Request**: User asked me to list all technology choices for verification. I provided a comprehensive list.

6. **User Corrections on Tech Stack**:
   - NOT using IBM Carbon → Will use eui.elastic.co (to verify feasibility)
   - Database: MongoDB preferred, PostgreSQL if necessary
   - Solver should exist - user asked why I didn't verify it
   - Need to validate optimization technique logic

7. **Process Agreement**: User established a step-by-step verification process:
   - I research/analyze
   - Present conclusions with evidence
   - User validates or requests correction
   - Move to next topic

8. **Solver Verification Started**: I began verifying the Python solver:
   - Found `.venv` with Python 3.13
   - Read `packages/solver/src/cli.py` - CLI entry point using stdin/stdout JSON
   - Read `packages/solver/src/types.py` - Pydantic models for solver I/O
   - Was about to read `solver.py` when power outage interrupted

Key files examined:
- `cli.py`: Entry point, reads JSON from stdin, calls solve(), outputs JSON
- `types.py`: Pydantic models defining SolverInput, SolverOutput, SolverEvent, etc.
- References HiGHS as solver name in diagnostics

The solver.py file exists but hasn't been read yet - this is where the actual MIP/HiGHS implementation would be.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   - User wants a complete, honest assessment of the MMS (Mileage Management System) project for Dubai Metro (Keolis MHI client)
   - Critical context: Project is AT RISK due to confusing SoW sent to client (AI-generated content caused confusion)
   - User needs to verify ALL technology choices and implementations - nothing should be assumed working
   - Step-by-step verification process: I research → present findings → user validates → next topic
   - RFQ is the ONLY reliable source of truth (from client)
   - Demo REQUIRES persistent database; Excel export is "nice to have"

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - MIP (Mixed Integer Programming) for maintenance scheduling optimization
   - HiGHS solver (open-source MIP solver)
   - RUC System (Recommended Usage Category): ACCELERATE/NOMINAL/DECELERATE
   - Mileage-based maintenance scheduling for 128 trains (79 Alstom + 49 Kinki Sharyo)
   - 12-year planning horizon
   - Resource leveling and workload balancing
   - UI: Elastic UI (eui.elastic.co) - NOT IBM Carbon (removed)
   - Database: MongoDB preferred, PostgreSQL as fallback
   - Python solver with Pydantic models
   - JSON stdin/stdout interface between solver and API

3. Files and Code Sections:
   - **`/Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/uptime/CLAUDE.md`** (MODIFIED)
     - Added critical project status warning
     - Marked all components as UNVERIFIED
     - Updated demo requirements (database mandatory, Excel nice-to-have)
     ```markdown
     ## ⚠️ CRITICAL PROJECT STATUS
     **THIS PROJECT IS AT RISK.** Much of the documentation and code was AI-generated without proper verification.
     **NOTHING IS ASSUMED WORKING UNTIL MANUALLY VERIFIED.**
     ```

   - **`/Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/uptime/docs/TECHNICAL_DEBT.md`** (MODIFIED)
     - Added critical warning about AI-generated content
     - Updated demo blockers: DATABASE, DATA FLOW, SOLVER verification
     ```markdown
     ## Demo Blockers (MANDATORY)
     | Item | Description | Status |
     | DATABASE | Persistent storage required | ❌ NOT DONE |
     | DATA FLOW | API → DB → Frontend working | ❌ NOT VERIFIED |
     | SOLVER | Actually solves problems | ❌ NOT VERIFIED |
     **Excel export is NICE TO HAVE, not required for demo.**
     ```

   - **`/Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/uptime/docs/internal/README.md`** (MODIFIED)
     - Added trust level warnings for each document
     - Highlighted that most docs are AI-generated

   - **`/Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/uptime/packages/solver/src/cli.py`** (READ)
     - CLI entry point for solver
     - Reads JSON from stdin, calls solve(), outputs JSON to stdout
     ```python
     def main() -> int:
         input_json = sys.stdin.read()
         input_data = json.loads(input_json)
         solver_input = SolverInput.model_validate(input_data)
         result = solve(solver_input)
         output_json = result.model_dump_json(indent=2)
         print(output_json)
     ```

   - **`/Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/uptime/packages/solver/src/types.py`** (READ)
     - Pydantic models for solver I/O
     - Key classes: SolverInput, SolverOutput, SolverEvent, SolverStatus, EventAssignment
     - References HiGHS in SolverDiagnostics
     ```python
     class SolverStatus(str, Enum):
         OPTIMAL = "OPTIMAL"
         FEASIBLE = "FEASIBLE"
         INFEASIBLE = "INFEASIBLE"
         TIMEOUT = "TIMEOUT"
         ERROR = "ERROR"
     ```

   - **`/Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/uptime/packages/solver/src/solver.py`** (EXISTS but NOT YET READ)
     - This is where the actual MIP/HiGHS implementation should be

4. Errors and fixes:
   - No code errors encountered
   - User correction: IBM Carbon should be removed, use Elastic UI instead
   - User correction: Database is REQUIRED for demo, not optional
   - User correction: Excel export moved to "nice to have"
   - User feedback: I should have verified solver exists by reading files, not just documenting

5. Problem Solving:
   - Identified that project documentation was largely AI-generated and unreliable
   - Updated critical documents to reflect true project status
   - Established verification process with user
   - Started verifying solver existence - found Python files exist, types defined, but actual solver.py not yet read

6. All user messages:
   - "Liste todos os arquivos na pasta ./docs recursivamente"
   - "Leia os arquivos e entenda o projeto, é essencial que você tenha uma visão completa e detalhada de cada ponto e descricao documentado"
   - "Altere os documentos se necessário: Precisamos usar database persistente desde a demo - export excel na demo é 'nice to have'. - Grande parte de tudo que está descrito no projeto foi gerado por AI e por isso tivemos uma SoW confusa e estamos em risco de perder o projeto, então é extremamente crítico e essencial que tudo seja revisto, nada do que está marcado como pronto deve ser assumido como pronto, tudo que está documentado precisa ser checado criteriosamente e minuciosamente - apenas a RFQ e o SoW são confiaveis para nós, a RFQ é nosso guia/source of truth, pois ela foi enviada pelo cliente, o SoW é um problema, mas é 'real' pois já enviamos ao cliente. Preciso que você por ora, descreva detalhadamente tudo o que sabe sobre o projeto, vou fazer perguntas e depois te passarei os proximos passos"
   - "Preciso que liste aqui mesmo no chat todas as escolhas tecnologicas, libs escolhidas, etc - e me aguarde verificar se houveram alucinacoes nas escolhas- desde lib de UI até técnicas para resolver os agendamentos (MIP, etc), etc..."
   - "não usaremos IBM carbon, remova as referencias; usaremos eui.elastic.co (verificar possibilidade), Database, se possível MongoDB, se necessário/não possível, PostGres; O solver deveria existir, verificar (você tem acesso, porque não verificou?); 'Técnica de otimização' validar a logica da escolha. Faremos passo a passo, você pergunta/pesquisa/pensa/analisa, eu analiso cada conclusão e confirmo ou requisito que você corrija ou faca outra coisa. Está claro? Tem dúvidas sobre como seguiremos?"
   - "A eletricidade havia ocilado aqui, continue."

7. Pending Tasks:
   - Read and verify `packages/solver/src/solver.py` to confirm HiGHS/MIP implementation
   - Validate optimization technique logic (MIP choice)
   - Verify Elastic UI (eui.elastic.co) feasibility
   - Verify MongoDB feasibility
   - Remove IBM Carbon references from documentation
   - Continue step-by-step verification of all technology choices

8. Current Work:
   I was in the middle of **STEP 1: Verifying the Python Solver exists and works**.
   - Confirmed `packages/solver/` directory exists with Python 3.13 venv
   - Read `cli.py` (entry point) and `types.py` (Pydantic models)
   - Found that `solver.py` exists via Glob
   - Was about to read `solver.py` to verify actual HiGHS/MIP implementation when power outage interrupted

9. Optional Next Step:
   Read `/Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/uptime/packages/solver/src/solver.py` to verify if HiGHS/MIP is actually implemented.
   
   User's exact instruction: "O solver deveria existir, verificar (você tem acesso, porque não verificou?)"
   
   My last action before interruption: `Glob` for `packages/solver/src/solver.py` returned the file exists. Next logical step is to read it..
Please continue the conversation from where we left it off without asking the user any further questions. Continue with the last task that you were asked to work on.

1→"""
     2→Main solver implementation using HiGHS MIP solver.
     3→"""
     4→
     5→import time
     6→from typing import Optional
     7→
     8→import highspy
     9→import numpy as np
    10→
    11→from .types import (
    12→    EventAssignment,
    13→    PeriodWorkload,
    14→    SolverDiagnostics,
    15→    SolverInput,
    16→    SolverMetrics,
    17→    SolverOutput,
    18→    SolverSolution,
    19→    SolverStatus,
    20→)
    21→
    22→
    23→def solve(input_data: SolverInput) -> SolverOutput:
    24→    """
    25→    Solve the maintenance scheduling problem using HiGHS MIP.
    26→
    27→    The optimization model:
    28→    - Decision variables: x[i,t] = 1 if event i is scheduled in period t
    29→    - Objective: minimize peak workload + workload variance + deviation from target
    30→    - Constraints:
    31→      - Each event assigned to exactly one period
    32→      - Events within their time window
    33→      - Workload per period <= capacity
    34→    """
    35→    start_time = time.perf_counter()
    36→
    37→    # Handle empty input
    38→    if len(input_data.events) == 0:
    39→        return _empty_solution(start_time)
    40→
    41→    try:
    42→        # Build and solve the MIP model
    43→        solution, metrics, diagnostics = _solve_mip(input_data, start_time)
    44→
    45→        if solution is None:
    46→            return SolverOutput(
    47→                status=SolverStatus.INFEASIBLE,
    48→                solution=None,
    49→                metrics=None,
    50→                diagnostics=diagnostics,
    51→            )
    52→
    53→        return SolverOutput(
    54→            status=SolverStatus.OPTIMAL,
    55→            solution=solution,
    56→            metrics=metrics,
    57→            diagnostics=diagnostics,
    58→        )
    59→
    60→    except Exception as e:
    61→        elapsed_ms = (time.perf_counter() - start_time) * 1000
    62→        return SolverOutput(
    63→            status=SolverStatus.ERROR,
    64→            solution=None,
    65→            metrics=None,
    66→            diagnostics=SolverDiagnostics(
    67→                solver_name="HiGHS",
    68→                solve_time_ms=elapsed_ms,
    69→                iterations=0,
    70→                warnings=[str(e)],
    71→            ),
    72→        )
    73→
    74→
    75→def _empty_solution(start_time: float) -> SolverOutput:
    76→    """Return solution for empty input."""
    77→    elapsed_ms = (time.perf_counter() - start_time) * 1000
    78→    return SolverOutput(
    79→        status=SolverStatus.OPTIMAL,
    80→        solution=SolverSolution(assignments=[], workload_profile=[]),
    81→        metrics=SolverMetrics(
    82→            objective_value=0,
    83→            peak_workload=0,
    84→            average_workload=0,
    85→            workload_variance=0,
    86→            workload_cv=0,
    87→            compliance_rate=1.0,
    88→            total_cost=0,
    89→        ),
    90→        diagnostics=SolverDiagnostics(
    91→            solver_name="HiGHS",
    92→            solve_time_ms=elapsed_ms,
    93→            iterations=0,
    94→        ),
    95→    )
    96→
    97→
    98→def _solve_mip(
    99→    input_data: SolverInput,
   100→    start_time: float,
   101→) -> tuple[Optional[SolverSolution], Optional[SolverMetrics], SolverDiagnostics]:
   102→    """Build and solve the MIP model."""
   103→    events = input_data.events
   104→    num_events = len(events)
   105→    num_periods = input_data.planning_horizon_weeks
   106→    capacity = input_data.constraints.weekly_man_hours
   107→
   108→    # Create HiGHS model
   109→    h = highspy.Highs()
   110→    h.setOptionValue("output_flag", False)
   111→    h.setOptionValue("time_limit", float(input_data.settings.timeout_seconds))
   112→    h.setOptionValue("mip_rel_gap", input_data.settings.gap_tolerance)
   113→
   114→    # Variable indices
   115→    # x[i,t] = 1 if event i is scheduled in period t
   116→    # Flattened: var_index = i * num_periods + t
   117→    num_x_vars = num_events * num_periods
   118→
   119→    # w[t] = workload in period t (continuous)
   120→    # peak = maximum workload (continuous)
   121→    # dev[i] = |assigned_period - target_period| for event i (continuous, for linearization)
   122→
   123→    # Total variables: x (binary) + w (continuous) + peak (continuous)
   124→    x_start = 0
   125→    w_start = num_x_vars
   126→    peak_idx = w_start + num_periods
   127→
   128→    num_vars = peak_idx + 1
   129→
   130→    # Variable bounds and types
   131→    lower = np.zeros(num_vars)
   132→    upper = np.ones(num_x_vars).tolist() + [capacity] * num_periods + [capacity]
   133→    upper = np.array(upper)
   134→
   135→    # Variable types: binary for x, continuous for rest
   136→    integrality = np.zeros(num_vars, dtype=np.int32)
   137→    integrality[:num_x_vars] = 1  # Binary
   138→
   139→    # Set variable bounds for x based on time windows
   140→    for i, event in enumerate(events):
   141→        for t in range(num_periods):
   142→            var_idx = x_start + i * num_periods + t
   143→            if t < event.earliest_period or t > event.latest_period:
   144→                upper[var_idx] = 0  # Force to 0 if outside window
   145→
   146→    # Objective: minimize peak + variance proxy
   147→    # We'll use peak minimization as primary objective
   148→    # Deviation from target is handled via soft penalty
   149→    obj_weight = input_data.objective
   150→    cost = np.zeros(num_vars)
   151→
   152→    # Peak workload (to minimize)
   153→    cost[peak_idx] = obj_weight.peak_workload_weight * 10.0
   154→
   155→    # Deviation penalty: prefer scheduling close to target
   156→    for i, event in enumerate(events):
   157→        for t in range(num_periods):
   158→            var_idx = x_start + i * num_periods + t
   159→            deviation = abs(t - event.target_period)
   160→            cost[var_idx] = obj_weight.compliance_weight * deviation * 0.1
   161→
   162→    # Add variables to model
   163→    h.addVars(num_vars, lower.tolist(), upper.tolist())
   164→
   165→    # Set costs using numpy arrays with correct types
   166→    indices = np.arange(num_vars, dtype=np.int32)
   167→    h.changeColsCost(num_vars, indices, cost.astype(np.float64))
   168→
   169→    # Set integrality for binary variables
   170→    x_indices = np.arange(num_x_vars, dtype=np.int32)
   171→    h.changeColsIntegrality(num_x_vars, x_indices, integrality[:num_x_vars])
   172→
   173→    # Constraint 1: Each event assigned to exactly one period
   174→    for i in range(num_events):
   175→        row_indices = [x_start + i * num_periods + t for t in range(num_periods)]
   176→        row_values = [1.0] * num_periods
   177→        h.addRow(1.0, 1.0, len(row_indices), row_indices, row_values)
   178→
   179→    # Constraint 2: Workload calculation w[t] = sum_i (x[i,t] * man_hours[i])
   180→    for t in range(num_periods):
   181→        row_indices = []
   182→        row_values = []
   183→        for i, event in enumerate(events):
   184→            row_indices.append(x_start + i * num_periods + t)
   185→            row_values.append(event.man_hours)
   186→        row_indices.append(w_start + t)
   187→        row_values.append(-1.0)
   188→        h.addRow(0.0, 0.0, len(row_indices), row_indices, row_values)
   189→
   190→    # Constraint 3: Workload capacity w[t] <= capacity
   191→    for t in range(num_periods):
   192→        h.addRow(-highspy.kHighsInf, capacity, 1, [w_start + t], [1.0])
   193→
   194→    # Constraint 4: Peak >= w[t] for all t
   195→    for t in range(num_periods):
   196→        row_indices = [peak_idx, w_start + t]
   197→        row_values = [1.0, -1.0]
   198→        h.addRow(0.0, highspy.kHighsInf, 2, row_indices, row_values)
   199→
   200→    # Solve
   201→    h.run()
   202→
   203→    # Get solve statistics
   204→    info = h.getInfo()
   205→    elapsed_ms = (time.perf_counter() - start_time) * 1000
   206→
   207→    # Calculate iterations safely (handle negative or missing values)
   208→    simplex_iters = getattr(info, "simplex_iteration_count", 0) or 0
   209→    ipm_iters = getattr(info, "ipm_iteration_count", 0) or 0
   210→    total_iters = max(0, int(simplex_iters) + int(ipm_iters))
   211→
   212→    diagnostics = SolverDiagnostics(
   213→        solver_name="HiGHS",
   214→        solve_time_ms=elapsed_ms,
   215→        iterations=total_iters,
   216→        mip_gap=info.mip_gap if hasattr(info, "mip_gap") else None,
   217→        node_count=max(0, int(info.mip_node_count)) if hasattr(info, "mip_node_count") else None,
   218→    )
   219→
   220→    # Check status
   221→    status = h.getModelStatus()
   222→    if status not in [highspy.HighsModelStatus.kOptimal, highspy.HighsModelStatus.kNotset]:
   223→        if status == highspy.HighsModelStatus.kInfeasible:
   224→            return None, None, diagnostics
   225→        # For timeout, return best found
   226→        if info.primal_solution_status != highspy.HighsSolutionStatus.kSolutionStatusFeasible:
   227→            return None, None, diagnostics
   228→
   229→    # Extract solution
   230→    solution_values = h.getSolution().col_value
   231→
   232→    # Build assignments
   233→    assignments = []
   234→    for i, event in enumerate(events):
   235→        for t in range(num_periods):
   236→            var_idx = x_start + i * num_periods + t
   237→            if solution_values[var_idx] > 0.5:
   238→                assignments.append(
   239→                    EventAssignment(
   240→                        event_id=event.id,
   241→                        assigned_period=t,
   242→                        deviation=t - event.target_period,
   243→                    )
   244→                )
   245→                break
   246→
   247→    # Build workload profile
   248→    workload_profile = []
   249→    for t in range(num_periods):
   250→        man_hours = solution_values[w_start + t]
   251→        event_count = sum(
   252→            1 for a in assignments if a.assigned_period == t
   253→        )
   254→        workload_profile.append(
   255→            PeriodWorkload(
   256→                period=t,
   257→                man_hours=man_hours,
   258→                event_count=event_count,
   259→                utilization=man_hours / capacity if capacity > 0 else 0,
   260→            )
   261→        )
   262→
   263→    solution = SolverSolution(
   264→        assignments=assignments,
   265→        workload_profile=workload_profile,
   266→    )
   267→
   268→    # Calculate metrics
   269→    workloads = [pw.man_hours for pw in workload_profile]
   270→    peak = max(workloads) if workloads else 0
   271→    avg = np.mean(workloads) if workloads else 0
   272→    variance = np.var(workloads) if workloads else 0
   273→    cv = np.std(workloads) / avg if avg > 0 else 0
   274→
   275→    on_target = sum(1 for a in assignments if abs(a.deviation) <= 2)
   276→    compliance_rate = on_target / len(assignments) if assignments else 1.0
   277→
   278→    # Calculate total cost
   279→    event_map = {e.id: e for e in events}
   280→    mt_map = {mt.id: mt for mt in input_data.maintenance_types}
   281→    total_cost = sum(
   282→        mt_map.get(event_map[a.event_id].maintenance_type_id, type("", (), {"estimated_cost": 0})).estimated_cost
   283→        for a in assignments
   284→        if a.event_id in event_map
   285→    )
   286→
   287→    metrics = SolverMetrics(
   288→        objective_value=solution_values[peak_idx] if peak_idx < len(solution_values) else peak,
   289→        peak_workload=peak,
   290→        average_workload=float(avg),
   291→        workload_variance=float(variance),
   292→        workload_cv=float(cv),
   293→        compliance_rate=compliance_rate,
   294→        total_cost=float(total_cost),
   295→    )
   296→
   297→    return solution, metrics, diagnostics
   298→

"""
Main solver implementation using HiGHS MIP solver.
"""

import time
from typing import Optional

import highspy
import numpy as np

from .types import (
    EventAssignment,
    PeriodWorkload,
    SolverDiagnostics,
    SolverInput,
    SolverMetrics,
    SolverOutput,
    SolverSolution,
    SolverStatus,
)


def solve(input_data: SolverInput) -> SolverOutput:
    """
    Solve the maintenance scheduling problem using HiGHS MIP.

    The optimization model:
    - Decision variables: x[i,t] = 1 if event i is scheduled in period t
    - Objective: minimize peak workload + workload variance + deviation from target
    - Constraints:
      - Each event assigned to exactly one period
      - Events within their time window
      - Workload per period <= capacity
    """
    start_time = time.perf_counter()

    # Handle empty input
    if len(input_data.events) == 0:
        return _empty_solution(start_time)

    try:
        # Build and solve the MIP model
        solution, metrics, diagnostics = _solve_mip(input_data, start_time)

        if solution is None:
            return SolverOutput(
                status=SolverStatus.INFEASIBLE,
                solution=None,
                metrics=None,
                diagnostics=diagnostics,
            )

        return SolverOutput(
            status=SolverStatus.OPTIMAL,
            solution=solution,
            metrics=metrics,
            diagnostics=diagnostics,
        )

    except Exception as e:
        elapsed_ms = (time.perf_counter() - start_time) * 1000
        return SolverOutput(
            status=SolverStatus.ERROR,
            solution=None,
            metrics=None,
            diagnostics=SolverDiagnostics(
                solver_name="HiGHS",
                solve_time_ms=elapsed_ms,
                iterations=0,
                warnings=[str(e)],
            ),
        )


def _empty_solution(start_time: float) -> SolverOutput:
    """Return solution for empty input."""
    elapsed_ms = (time.perf_counter() - start_time) * 1000
    return SolverOutput(
        status=SolverStatus.OPTIMAL,
        solution=SolverSolution(assignments=[], workload_profile=[]),
        metrics=SolverMetrics(
            objective_value=0,
            peak_workload=0,
            average_workload=0,
            workload_variance=0,
            workload_cv=0,
            compliance_rate=1.0,
            total_cost=0,
        ),
        diagnostics=SolverDiagnostics(
            solver_name="HiGHS",
            solve_time_ms=elapsed_ms,
            iterations=0,
        ),
    )


def _solve_mip(
    input_data: SolverInput,
    start_time: float,
) -> tuple[Optional[SolverSolution], Optional[SolverMetrics], SolverDiagnostics]:
    """Build and solve the MIP model."""
    events = input_data.events
    num_events = len(events)
    num_periods = input_data.planning_horizon_weeks
    capacity = input_data.constraints.weekly_man_hours

    # Create HiGHS model
    h = highspy.Highs()
    h.setOptionValue("output_flag", False)
    h.setOptionValue("time_limit", float(input_data.settings.timeout_seconds))
    h.setOptionValue("mip_rel_gap", input_data.settings.gap_tolerance)

    # Variable indices
    # x[i,t] = 1 if event i is scheduled in period t
    # Flattened: var_index = i * num_periods + t
    num_x_vars = num_events * num_periods

    # w[t] = workload in period t (continuous)
    # peak = maximum workload (continuous)
    # dev[i] = |assigned_period - target_period| for event i (continuous, for linearization)

    # Total variables: x (binary) + w (continuous) + peak (continuous)
    x_start = 0
    w_start = num_x_vars
    peak_idx = w_start + num_periods

    num_vars = peak_idx + 1

    # Variable bounds and types
    lower = np.zeros(num_vars)
    upper = np.ones(num_x_vars).tolist() + [capacity] * num_periods + [capacity]
    upper = np.array(upper)

    # Variable types: binary for x, continuous for rest
    integrality = np.zeros(num_vars, dtype=np.int32)
    integrality[:num_x_vars] = 1  # Binary

    # Set variable bounds for x based on time windows
    for i, event in enumerate(events):
        for t in range(num_periods):
            var_idx = x_start + i * num_periods + t
            if t < event.earliest_period or t > event.latest_period:
                upper[var_idx] = 0  # Force to 0 if outside window

    # Objective: minimize peak + variance proxy
    # We'll use peak minimization as primary objective
    # Deviation from target is handled via soft penalty
    obj_weight = input_data.objective
    cost = np.zeros(num_vars)

    # Peak workload (to minimize)
    cost[peak_idx] = obj_weight.peak_workload_weight * 10.0

    # Deviation penalty: prefer scheduling close to target
    for i, event in enumerate(events):
        for t in range(num_periods):
            var_idx = x_start + i * num_periods + t
            deviation = abs(t - event.target_period)
            cost[var_idx] = obj_weight.compliance_weight * deviation * 0.1

    # Add variables to model
    h.addVars(num_vars, lower.tolist(), upper.tolist())

    # Set costs using numpy arrays with correct types
    indices = np.arange(num_vars, dtype=np.int32)
    h.changeColsCost(num_vars, indices, cost.astype(np.float64))

    # Set integrality for binary variables
    x_indices = np.arange(num_x_vars, dtype=np.int32)
    h.changeColsIntegrality(num_x_vars, x_indices, integrality[:num_x_vars])

    # Constraint 1: Each event assigned to exactly one period
    for i in range(num_events):
        row_indices = [x_start + i * num_periods + t for t in range(num_periods)]
        row_values = [1.0] * num_periods
        h.addRow(1.0, 1.0, len(row_indices), row_indices, row_values)

    # Constraint 2: Workload calculation w[t] = sum_i (x[i,t] * man_hours[i])
    for t in range(num_periods):
        row_indices = []
        row_values = []
        for i, event in enumerate(events):
            row_indices.append(x_start + i * num_periods + t)
            row_values.append(event.man_hours)
        row_indices.append(w_start + t)
        row_values.append(-1.0)
        h.addRow(0.0, 0.0, len(row_indices), row_indices, row_values)

    # Constraint 3: Workload capacity w[t] <= capacity
    for t in range(num_periods):
        h.addRow(-highspy.kHighsInf, capacity, 1, [w_start + t], [1.0])

    # Constraint 4: Peak >= w[t] for all t
    for t in range(num_periods):
        row_indices = [peak_idx, w_start + t]
        row_values = [1.0, -1.0]
        h.addRow(0.0, highspy.kHighsInf, 2, row_indices, row_values)

    # Solve
    h.run()

    # Get solve statistics
    info = h.getInfo()
    elapsed_ms = (time.perf_counter() - start_time) * 1000

    # Calculate iterations safely (handle negative or missing values)
    simplex_iters = getattr(info, "simplex_iteration_count", 0) or 0
    ipm_iters = getattr(info, "ipm_iteration_count", 0) or 0
    total_iters = max(0, int(simplex_iters) + int(ipm_iters))

    diagnostics = SolverDiagnostics(
        solver_name="HiGHS",
        solve_time_ms=elapsed_ms,
        iterations=total_iters,
        mip_gap=info.mip_gap if hasattr(info, "mip_gap") else None,
        node_count=max(0, int(info.mip_node_count)) if hasattr(info, "mip_node_count") else None,
    )

    # Check status
    status = h.getModelStatus()
    if status not in [highspy.HighsModelStatus.kOptimal, highspy.HighsModelStatus.kNotset]:
        if status == highspy.HighsModelStatus.kInfeasible:
            return None, None, diagnostics
        # For timeout, return best found
        if info.primal_solution_status != highspy.HighsSolutionStatus.kSolutionStatusFeasible:
            return None, None, diagnostics

    # Extract solution
    solution_values = h.getSolution().col_value

    # Build assignments
    assignments = []
    for i, event in enumerate(events):
        for t in range(num_periods):
            var_idx = x_start + i * num_periods + t
            if solution_values[var_idx] > 0.5:
                assignments.append(
                    EventAssignment(
                        event_id=event.id,
                        assigned_period=t,
                        deviation=t - event.target_period,
                    )
                )
                break

    # Build workload profile
    workload_profile = []
    for t in range(num_periods):
        man_hours = solution_values[w_start + t]
        event_count = sum(
            1 for a in assignments if a.assigned_period == t
        )
        workload_profile.append(
            PeriodWorkload(
                period=t,
                man_hours=man_hours,
                event_count=event_count,
                utilization=man_hours / capacity if capacity > 0 else 0,
            )
        )

    solution = SolverSolution(
        assignments=assignments,
        workload_profile=workload_profile,
    )

    # Calculate metrics
    workloads = [pw.man_hours for pw in workload_profile]
    peak = max(workloads) if workloads else 0
    avg = np.mean(workloads) if workloads else 0
    variance = np.var(workloads) if workloads else 0
    cv = np.std(workloads) / avg if avg > 0 else 0

    on_target = sum(1 for a in assignments if abs(a.deviation) <= 2)
    compliance_rate = on_target / len(assignments) if assignments else 1.0

    # Calculate total cost
    event_map = {e.id: e for e in events}
    mt_map = {mt.id: mt for mt in input_data.maintenance_types}
    total_cost = sum(
        mt_map.get(event_map[a.event_id].maintenance_type_id, type("", (), {"estimated_cost": 0})).estimated_cost
        for a in assignments
        if a.event_id in event_map
    )

    metrics = SolverMetrics(
        objective_value=solution_values[peak_idx] if peak_idx < len(solution_values) else peak,
        peak_workload=peak,
        average_workload=float(avg),
        workload_variance=float(variance),
        workload_cv=float(cv),
        compliance_rate=compliance_rate,
        total_cost=float(total_cost),
    )

    return solution, metrics, diagnostics

Develop a solution that allows the maintenance and operations team to efficiently plan and schedule 
preventative maintenance activities for two distinct rolling stock fleets, consisting of 49 and 79 trains 
each (a total of 128 trains), while fulfilling operational requirements across the network. The system 
should offer an automated, data-driven, and real-time mileage management approach, considering 
different preventive maintenance activities, current resources, and operational needs. The requirements 
can be divided into three main categories.

Maintenance Planning: Key requirement to meet the maintenance planning, 
●​ Fetching daily mileage for each train from a specific location. 
●​ Predicting maintenance schedules and dates based on real-time mileage data. 
●​ Ability to adjust the maintenance schedule based on available resources (man-hours), train 
availability, and maintenance tracks. 
●​ The software should be capable of providing short-term (weekly, monthly) and long-term 
(yearly 1-10) plans based on the fixed parameters (man-hours and tracks). 
●​ Offer guidance and warnings on mileage underrun or overrun, as they can impact overall 
mileage management. 
●​ Availability to perform simulations on various planning and scheduling options. 
 
Operation Planning: Key requirement to fulfil the operational planning 
●​ Recommend the train numbers to be used daily based on the fitness status and mileage. 
●​ Recommend the run assignment (mileage and the network) for each train. 
●​ Provide warnings and recommendations to correct or adjust fleet mileage by comparing the 
predicted mileage with the actual mileage. 
●​ Provide an interface between the maintenance and operations teams to communicate on 
train fitness and mileage management.  
             Resource Planning: Key requirement to fulfil the Resource planning 
●​ The resource planning should consider the manpower, spare costs and tracks available, 
which are fixed parameters. 
●​ The maintenance planning should consider the fixed parameters and avoid overutilization or 
underutilization of the resources throughout the contract period (12 years) 
●​ The software should be able to provide the maintenance costing for each fleet based on the 
mileage and maintenance plan. 


## Page 2

●​ It should be able to provide different models or strategies in mileage management reflecting 
the changes in total maintenance costs in the short term and long term. 
2.​ Background 
●​
At present, no dedicated tools are available for mileage management. 
●​
The existing platform, IBM Maximo, primarily serves as an asset management tool and is 
not specifically for managing mileage. 
●​
Currently, mileage management is being managed manually through Excel. 
●​
Existing setup currently depends on raw mileage data that is stored and collected 
manually (folder driven mileage data), which requires validation and system integration. 
●​
Different fleet types and importantly Increasing fleet size necessitate a centralized, 
automated solution to ensure better scheduling and compliance. 
●​
Limited integration between maintenance and operations on mileage decisions. 
●​
Risk of non-compliance due to mileage underrun/overrun as it is performed manually. 
●​
Lack of predictive simulations to evaluate planning options. 
●​
Insufficient visibility of resources vs. actual utilization. 
●​
Manual handling of data from multiple sources resulting in delays and inefficiencies. 
 
3.​ Software’s core deliverables 
The Mileage Management shall be capable of integrating, processing, and utilizing inputs from 
maintenance, operations, and other required input, ensuring an automated, data-driven, and rule-based 
approach. 
●​
Daily Mileage Data 
●​
Maintenance Plans 
●​
Maintenance Resources: 
o​ Man-hours (fixed parameter). 
o​ Track allocation for maintenance activities. 
o​ Spare parts availability and baseline cost. 
●​
Fleet Identification: Train IDs and fleet classification (Kinki Sharyo / Alstom) 
●​
Fleet availability and Train Fitness Status 
●​
Mileage Balance Monitoring based on recommended run assignment. 
●​
Daily train deployment requirements across the network. 
●​
Adjustments between predicted vs. actual mileage utilization. 
●​
Maintenance budget forecasting 
 
4.​ Software input 
●​ Train IDs and fleet type (Kinki Sharyo and Alstom) 
●​ Daily Mileage – Live data 
●​ Daily train fitness status 
●​ Daily operation requirement and command for each train run IDs 
●​ Maintenance plans (e.g. PM, overhauls) 


## Page 3

●​ Track availability 
●​ Man-hours per maintenance 
●​ Maintenance baseline cost per maintenance – material cost, service cost 
●​ Man-hour cost 
●​ Base Depot for each fleet (Identification of the designated maintenance depot for each 
fleet) 
 
5.​ Software output 
●​ Centralised dashboard – This should provide one high level customised output and other 
comprises complete data, mileage status 
●​ Current maintenance status of all trains in terms of mileage 
●​ Train run assignments (Optimised allocation of trains for daily service based on fitness 
and mileage balance) 
●​ Completed and upcoming PM/overhaul activities (Predicted schedule of upcoming 
preventive maintenance and overhauls) 
●​ Maintenance compliance status (Real-time tracking of adherence to maintenance 
requirements based on run assignments) 
●​ Monthly and yearly cost compliance (Comparison of actual vs. planned maintenance 
costs on a monthly and yearly basis) 
●​ Standard and custom report generation (PDF, Excel) 
●​ Simulation report of different models on mileage management 
●​ Email notification of the generated reports to the stakeholders 
 
6.​ User Interface 
●​ Web-based application (preferred) accessible on desktops and mobile devices. 
●​ Capability to integrate with MMS (Maximo IBM) 
●​ Data security and compliance with IT standards. 
●​ Scalable architecture to accommodate additional fleets and extended functionalities. 
●​ Customizable alerts/notifications 
●​ Full accessibility as an admin. 
 
7.​ Implementation support 
●​ Project implementation methodology and timeline. 
●​ Case studies or references of similar implementations. 
●​ Supply, installation, and commissioning of the Mileage Control Management software. 
●​ Warranty and post-deployment support, including upgradation as per regular software 
updates and needs. 
●​ Migration of existing maintenance data into the system. 
●​ User training and knowledge transfer (Comprehensive training sessions for system users 
to ensure smooth adoption) 


## Page 4

●​ Documentation (Detailed technical and user manuals covering all functionalities) 
 
8.​ Case studies 
 Example:  Workload (Man-hour projection required vs available): 
 
a) Without adjustment: 
 
 
b) With adjustment 
 
 
9.​ Phase deployment 
 
●​ Phase 1 – with regards to complete requirements as outlines above, phase 1 should have 
implementation stage to cover the mileage management and run assignments. 
●​ Phase 2 – This phase where the costing part is included and required output is driven. 


## Page 5

●​ Phase 3 – Inclusion of room for the additional fleet into the system for which system 
retains the capabilities to do so. Competence Management part to be added to the 
system which can regulate the competency of the user. 
●​ Phase 3 – To be defined (Future scope)

1→# Railway Rolling Stock Maintenance Scheduling: Research & Industry Practices
     2→
     3→> Comprehensive documentation for RAG system - Dubai Metro MMS Project
     4→> Last updated: December 2024
     5→
     6→## Table of Contents
     7→
     8→1. [Executive Summary](#executive-summary)
     9→2. [Problem Definition](#problem-definition)
    10→3. [Planning Hierarchy](#planning-hierarchy)
    11→4. [Optimization Techniques](#optimization-techniques)
    12→5. [Case Studies by Metro System](#case-studies-by-metro-system)
    13→6. [Technology & Systems](#technology--systems)
    14→7. [Academic Research](#academic-research)
    15→8. [Best Practices](#best-practices)
    16→9. [References](#references)
    17→
    18→---
    19→
    20→## Executive Summary
    21→
    22→Railway rolling stock maintenance scheduling is a complex optimization problem that combines:
    23→- **Resource allocation** (man-hours, tracks, depots)
    24→- **Time/mileage constraints** (preventive maintenance intervals)
    25→- **Operational requirements** (train availability, service levels)
    26→- **Cost optimization** (labor, materials, penalties)
    27→
    28→Key findings from our research:
    29→
    30→| Aspect | Industry Standard |
    31→|--------|------------------|
    32→| Planning horizons | Daily → Weekly → Monthly → Annual → Long-term (10+ years) |
    33→| Primary trigger | Mileage-based (evolving to condition-based) |
    34→| Common techniques | MIP, Genetic Algorithms, Large Neighborhood Search, Hybrid CP/MIP |
    35→| Software platforms | IBM Maximo, SAP EAM, Custom optimization engines |
    36→| Performance target | 99.9% train availability (Hong Kong MTR benchmark) |
    37→
    38→---
    39→
    40→## Problem Definition
    41→
    42→### The Core Scheduling Problem
    43→
    44→The railway maintenance scheduling problem involves:
    45→
    46→1. **Fleet Management**: Multiple train types with different maintenance requirements
    47→2. **Maintenance Types**: Varying intervals and durations
    48→   - Daily inspections
    49→   - Monthly checks (~15,000 km)
    50→   - Quarterly services (~45,000 km)
    51→   - Annual overhauls (~180,000 km)
    52→   - Major overhauls (4-6 years)
    53→
    54→3. **Resource Constraints**:
    55→   - Limited maintenance tracks
    56→   - Fixed workforce (man-hours per period)
    57→   - Depot capacity
    58→   - Spare parts availability
    59→
    60→4. **Objectives** (often conflicting):
    61→   - Minimize maintenance costs
    62→   - Maximize train availability
    63→   - Level resource utilization
    64→   - Comply with safety regulations
    65→
    66→### Problem Complexity
    67→
    68→According to research from [Wiley - Optimization of Annual Planned Rail Maintenance](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/mice.12764):
    69→
    70→> "Using the CPLEX MIP solver, researchers managed to solve only 62% of instances within 3 hours. Kiefer et al. (2018) formulated their problem as an MIP model but quickly found runtime limitations on larger instances, exceeding the 24-hour time limit."
    71→
    72→This demonstrates why heuristic and metaheuristic approaches are essential for real-world applications.
    73→
    74→---
    75→
    76→## Planning Hierarchy
    77→
    78→### Multi-Level Planning Structure
    79→
    80→Based on [JICA Urban Railways Handbook](https://www.jica.go.jp/english/activities/issues/transport/__icsFiles/afieldfile/2025/03/26/handbook_en_01.pdf) and industry practices:
    81→
    82→```
    83→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    84→│                     MASTER PLAN (Annual/Multi-Year)             │
    85→│  - Resource allocation across the contract period               │
    86→│  - Major overhaul scheduling                                    │
    87→│  - Budget planning                                              │
    88→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    89→│                     MONTHLY PLAN                                │
    90→│  - Medium-scale repairs allocation                              │
    91→│  - Workload balancing                                           │
    92→│  - Resource adjustment                                          │
    93→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    94→│                     WEEKLY PLAN                                 │
    95→│  - Detailed work orders                                         │
    96→│  - Staff rostering                                              │
    97→│  - Track allocation                                             │
    98→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    99→│                     DAILY PLAN                                  │
   100→│  - Execution scheduling                                         │
   101→│  - Real-time adjustments                                        │
   102→│  - Emergency response                                           │
   103→└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
   104→```
   105→
   106→### Tokyo Metro Approach
   107→
   108→From [JICA Rolling Stock Maintenance Manuals](https://openjicareport.jica.go.jp/pdf/12251708_02.pdf):
   109→
   110→> "Tokyo Metro employs an 'Equalizing' approach to secure the required work progress, equalize the maintenance workload, and enable rolling stock maintenance personnel to fulfill their duties."
   111→
   112→Key principles:
   113→- **Even workload distribution** across periods
   114→- **Mileage tracking** for devices requiring km-based maintenance
   115→- **Train allocation** at daily, monthly, and annual levels
   116→
   117→---
   118→
   119→## Optimization Techniques
   120→
   121→### 1. Mixed Integer Programming (MIP)
   122→
   123→**Strengths:**
   124→- Mathematically optimal solutions
   125→- Well-understood theory
   126→- Commercial solvers available (Gurobi, CPLEX)
   127→
   128→**Limitations:**
   129→- Scalability issues with large instances
   130→- Long computation times
   131→- Requires linearization of constraints
   132→
   133→**Example Application - Deutsche Bahn:**
   134→
   135→From [Deutsche Bahn Hypergraph Optimization Case Study](https://pubsonline.informs.org/doi/10.1287/inte.2020.1069):
   136→
   137→> "DB attributes annual savings of €74 million, an annual reduction of 34,000 tons of CO2 emissions, and the elimination of 600 coupling operations to the implementation of FEO (Fleet Employment Optimization)."
   138→
   139→### 2. Genetic Algorithms (GA)
   140→
   141→**Strengths:**
   142→- Handles complex, non-linear constraints
   143→- Good for multi-objective optimization
   144→- Scalable to large problems
   145→
   146→**Example Application - Hong Kong MTR:**
   147→
   148→From [CityU AI Projects - MTR Scheduling](https://www.cs.cityu.edu.hk/~hwchun/AIProjects/stories/allocation/mtrcscheduling/):
   149→
   150→> "The scheduling system is based on genetic algorithm and heuristic search, which work together with a rule-based system. The AI system improved maintenance efficiency by 50%, contributing to MTR's 99.9% on-time performance for 5 million daily passengers."
   151→
   152→### 3. Large Neighborhood Search (LNS)
   153→
   154→**Strengths:**
   155→- Effective for large-scale problems
   156→- Combines local search with diversification
   157→- Can escape local optima
   158→
   159→**Example Application - Vienna Tram:**
   160→
   161→From [ScienceDirect - Integrated Railway Planning](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0968090X16302340):
   162→
   163→> "Kiefer et al. developed a metaheuristic based on large neighborhood search after MIP exceeded 24-hour time limits. Testing on the Vienna tram network—the largest instance in literature—showed effective results."
   164→
   165→### 4. Hybrid Constraint Programming / MIP
   166→
   167→**Strengths:**
   168→- CP generates feasible solutions quickly
   169→- MIP improves solution quality
   170→- Best of both worlds
   171→
   172→**Example Application - Danish Railways:**
   173→
   174→From [ScienceDirect - Hybrid CP/MIP Framework](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0377221717307646):
   175→
   176→> "Whereas using a general purpose MIP solver is only able to solve instances over a two-week planning horizon, the hybrid framework generates good results for problem instances over an eight-week period."
   177→
   178→### 5. Greedy Metaheuristics
   179→
   180→**Strengths:**
   181→- Fast computation
   182→- Good initial solutions
   183→- Easy to implement
   184→
   185→**Example Application - Netherlands ProRail:**
   186→
   187→From [Wiley - Annual Rail Maintenance Optimization](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/mice.12764):
   188→
   189→> "A hybrid algorithm using evolution strategy and a greedy algorithm was created and shown to outperform all other algorithms, achieving a two-thirds decrease in costs compared to human scheduler solutions."
   190→
   191→### Technique Comparison Matrix
   192→
   193→| Technique | Problem Size | Solution Quality | Speed | Implementation |
   194→|-----------|--------------|------------------|-------|----------------|
   195→| MIP | Small-Medium | Optimal | Slow | Complex |
   196→| Genetic Algorithm | Large | Good | Medium | Moderate |
   197→| Large Neighborhood Search | Large | Good | Fast | Moderate |
   198→| Hybrid CP/MIP | Medium-Large | Very Good | Medium | Complex |
   199→| Greedy Heuristics | Any | Acceptable | Very Fast | Simple |
   200→
   201→---
   202→
   203→## Case Studies by Metro System
   204→
   205→### 1. Metrô São Paulo (In-House Innovation)
   206→*   **Strategy**: **Build (Internal)**.
   207→*   **System**: **SMA (Sistema de Monitoramento de Ativos)**.
   208→*   **Key Feature**: Developed entirely by internal engineering teams. Focuses on "Maintenance 5.0" with heavy use of IoT sensors for predictive failure detection.
   209→*   **Why**: To avoid vendor lock-in and ensure the system fits their specific operational reality perfectly.
   210→
   211→### 2. London Underground (The "Big ERP" Approach)
   212→*   **Strategy**: **Buy (Commercial EAM)**.
   213→*   **System**: **IBM Maximo**.
   214→*   **Key Feature**: Consolidated 60+ legacy systems into one massive Maximo instance.
   215→*   **Limitation**: While great for data storage and workflow, they still struggle with the complex *optimization* of schedules, often requiring custom add-ons or manual intervention for the "hard math".
   216→
   217→### 3. Tokyo Metro (Vendor Integrated)
   218→*   **Strategy**: **Vendor Ecosystem**.
   219→*   **System**: Hitachi **REFMa** / Toshiba Systems.
   220→*   **Key Feature**: Tight integration where the train manufacturer provides the maintenance software. The "Equalizing" methodology is strict, leveling workload across the year to ensure constant workforce utilization.
   221→
   222→### 4. Paris Métro - RATP (Commercialized Internal Tool)
   223→*   **Strategy**: **Build -> Sell**.
   224→*   **System**: **Maint'Up**.
   225→*   **Key Feature**: RATP Dev developed their own SaaS solution to manage their assets and now uses it as a selling point when bidding for operations contracts globally.
   226→
   227→### 5. Hong Kong MTR (AI Pioneer)
   228→*   **Strategy**: **Hybrid (ERP + AI)**.
   229→*   **System**: **IBM Maximo + Custom AI Engine**.
   230→*   **Key Feature**: Uses Maximo for the database but built a famous "Genetic Algorithm" engine (with City University of HK) to handle the actual scheduling. This AI engine is credited with their 99.9% reliability.
   231→
   232→### 6. Dubai Metro (RTA / Keolis MHI)
   233→
   234→**Current State:**
   235→- 128 trains (79 Alstom + 49 Kinki Sharyo)
   236→- Manual mileage management via Excel
   237→- IBM Maximo for asset management (not mileage-specific)
   238→
   239→**Recent Innovations:**
   240→
   241→From [RTA ARIIS System Launch](https://www.mediaoffice.ae/en/news/2025/june/12-06/rta-rolls-out-the-automated-rail-infrastructure-inspection-system-in-dubai-metro):
   242→
   243→> "The Automated Rail Infrastructure Inspection System (ARIIS) is expected to enhance safety by reducing manual inspections by up to 70%, improving infrastructure condition assessment by 40%, and reducing inspection times by 75%."
   244→
   245→From [Parsons RIMMS Project](https://www.parsons.com/project/rail-infrastructure-maintenance-management-system-rimms-dubai-united-arab-emirates-uae/):
   246→
   247→> "RIMMS helps the client move from a reactive approach to maintenance to a proactive, integrated approach informed by data."
   248→
   249→### 2. Tokyo Metro / JR East (Japan)
   250→
   251→**Key Features:**
   252→- ATOS (Autonomous Transport Operation System)
   253→- Integrated control center
   254→- Equalizing maintenance approach
   255→
   256→From [Hitachi ATOS Case Study](https://social-innovation.hitachi.en-us/case_studies/atos/):
   257→
   258→> "JR East implements an incredibly congested schedule of up to 10,000 trains per day. ATOS not only streamlines transport operation control work but also manages maintenance work procedures."
   259→
   260→**Maintenance Philosophy:**
   261→- Even workload distribution
   262→- Mileage-based tracking (wheel grinding, brakes)
   263→- Fixed annual allocation across periods
   264→
   265→### 3. London Underground (TfL)
   266→
   267→**Current Approach:**
   268→- IBM Maximo for 10,000+ technicians
   269→- Predictive maintenance using data analytics
   270→- Condition-based monitoring transition
   271→
   272→From [Computerworld - TfL Predictive Analytics](https://www.computerworld.com/article/1655414/how-tfl-is-using-predictive-analytics-to-keep-the-underground-moving.html):
   273→
   274→> "A project currently in production on the Central line analyzes events to predict when a motor is soon to fail. The expected savings from this is approximately £3 million per year."
   275→
   276→**Key Statistics:**
   277→- 59% of TfL's budget goes to asset maintenance
   278→- 50% of delays caused by asset problems
   279→- £300 million potential savings over train lifecycle
   280→
   281→From [TfL Asset Management Strategy](https://content.tfl.gov.uk/lu-asset-management-strategy.pdf):
   282→
   283→> "Maintenance regimes will be optimized to transfer maintenance periodicities from a calendar/service day based approach to a condition and usage basis."
   284→
   285→### 4. Deutsche Bahn (Germany)
   286→
   287→**Key Innovations:**
   288→
   289→From [KONUX Partnership](https://www.konux.com/konux-receives-another-significant-order-from-deutsche-bahn/):
   290→
   291→> "Deutsche Bahn has achieved a cost reduction of 25% through minimization of downtime and maximization of performance using predictive maintenance."
   292→
   293→**DIANA Platform:**
   294→- 40,000+ turnout drive sensors connected
   295→- Real-time condition monitoring
   296→- Predictive maintenance scheduling
   297→
   298→**FEO System (Fleet Employment Optimization):**
   299→- Hypergraph-based optimization
   300→- €74 million annual savings
   301→- 34,000 tons CO2 reduction
   302→- Collaboration with Zuse Institute Berlin (11 years)
   303→
   304→### 5. SNCF (France)
   305→
   306→**Scale:**
   307→- 1,100 CBM-enabled (Condition-Based Maintenance) trainsets
   308→- 2,500 equipped for remote diagnostics
   309→- 8,000+ variables per train analyzed
   310→- 2,000 real-time variables
   311→
   312→From [SNCF Predictive Maintenance](https://www.sncf.com/en/network-expertise/rolling-stock-division/how-predictive-maintenance-improves-rail-travel):
   313→
   314→> "After over 8 years of development, predictive maintenance is now a reality at SNCF. SNCF is a global leader in predictive maintenance for rail."
   315→
   316→**Academic Research:**
   317→
   318→From [Two-phase heuristic for SNCF](https://hal.science/hal-01936483v1/document):
   319→
   320→> "The first phase solves a train assignment problem by combining a greedy heuristic procedure with integer programming. The second phase schedules train movements while minimizing cancelled arrivals and departures."
   321→
   322→### 6. Netherlands Railways (NS) / ProRail
   323→
   324→**Unique Approach:**
   325→
   326→From [The Netherlands Track Maintenance Safety](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/220249901_The_Netherlands_Schedules_Track_Maintenance_to_Improve_Track_Workers'_Safety):
   327→
   328→> "Researchers developed a two-step method of constructing a four-week maintenance schedule in which each working zone of the main lines is closed to trains at night exactly once. Such a schedule is unique in Europe."
   329→
   330→**Key Statistics:**
   331→- 7,000+ km of railway track
   332→- 165 million kilometers driven by passenger trains annually
   333→- 21 maintenance areas with dedicated contractors
   334→
   335→**Research Contribution:**
   336→
   337→From [Operations Research at Netherlands Railways](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43069-024-00342-8):
   338→
   339→> "A joint project with ProRail developed an optimization approach for adjusting the Basic Days timetable up to 2 months before operations due to planned maintenance works."
   340→
   341→### 7. Singapore MRT (SMRT)
   342→
   343→**Key Features:**
   344→- 83 km, 48 stations
   345→- 77 trains peak / 41 off-peak
   346→- Six depots including Bishan (central), Changi, Ulu Pandan
   347→
   348→From [SMRT Train Operator Scheduling](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1023/A:1016002910561):
   349→
   350→> "An optimization-based approach using bipartite matching for night duties and tabu search for day duties automates train-operator scheduling at SMRT."
   351→
   352→**Depot 4.0 Modernization:**
   353→
   354→From [SMRT Depot 4.0](https://www.smrt.com.sg/news-publications/newsroom/media-releases/from-strength-to-strength-building-a-resilient-future/):
   355→
   356→> "SMRT's Depot 4.0 at Bishan will double train overhaul capacity from two to four trains per month, setting a benchmark for future depot design."
   357→
   358→### 8. Hong Kong MTR
   359→
   360→**World-Class Performance:**
   361→- 10 subway lines, 152 stations
   362→- 5 million passengers daily
   363→- 99.9% on-time performance
   364→- 2,600 engineering works weekly
   365→- 10,000 maintenance personnel
   366→
   367→From [AAAI Award-Winning AI System](https://cdn.aaai.org/AAAI/2005/IAAI05-003.pdf):
   368→
   369→> "The AI Engine improved maintenance efficiency by 50%, allowing more works to be performed within the limited 4-5 hour nightly maintenance window."
   370→
   371→**Awards:**
   372→- 2014 AAAI Innovative Applications Award (Deployed Applications)
   373→- 2005 APICTA Best Industrial Application Award
   374→
   375→---
   376→
   377→## Technology & Systems
   378→
   379→### Enterprise Asset Management (EAM)
   380→
   381→#### IBM Maximo
   382→
   383→From [IBM Maximo for TfL](https://www.ibm.com/case-studies/transport-for-london):
   384→
   385→> "Transport for London keeps the public moving safely, reliably and sustainably when it centralizes its maintenance efforts on IBM Maximo software."
   386→
   387→**Key Capabilities:**
   388→- Work order management
   389→- Asset lifecycle tracking
   390→- Integration with IoT sensors
   391→- Predictive maintenance analytics
   392→- Mobile workforce management
   393→
   394→**Market Position:**
   395→- ~35 years in market
   396→- Industry-specific modules (Transportation, Rail, Aviation)
   397→- Native SAP/Oracle integration
   398→
   399→#### SAP EAM
   400→
   401→**Strengths:**
   402→- Tight ERP integration
   403→- Financial/procurement alignment
   404→- S/4HANA platform
   405→
   406→**Comparison:**
   407→
   408→From [SelectHub - IBM Maximo vs SAP](https://www.selecthub.com/eam/ibm-maximo-and-sap/):
   409→
   410→> "IBM Maximo has deep maintenance functions and industry-specific functionality for various verticals. SAP EAM is deeply embedded within the SAP ERP ecosystem and preferred for seamless financial integration."
   411→
   412→### Predictive Maintenance Technologies
   413→
   414→#### Sensors and IoT
   415→
   416→- **Temperature sensors**: Motor, bearing monitoring
   417→- **Vibration sensors**: Wheel, axle condition
   418→- **Pressure sensors**: Brake system health
   419→- **LiDAR**: Track geometry inspection
   420→- **Cameras**: Visual inspection automation
   421→
   422→#### AI/ML Approaches
   423→
   424→From [TfL Predictive Maintenance](https://techinsight.net/2022/04/predictive-maintenance-london-underground/):
   425→
   426→> "AI helps schedule maintenance during non-peak hours, minimizing disruptions to passenger services by predicting when components will fail."
   427→
   428→### Optimization Solvers
   429→
   430→| Solver | Type | Strengths | Used By |
   431→|--------|------|-----------|---------|
   432→| Gurobi | MIP | Fast, scalable | Deutsche Bahn |
   433→| CPLEX | MIP | Industry standard | Academic research |
   434→| CP Optimizer | CP | Scheduling-specific | Danish Railways |
   435→| OR-Tools | Open source | Flexible | Various |
   436→| Custom GA | Metaheuristic | Problem-specific | Hong Kong MTR |
   437→
   438→---
   439→
   440→## Academic Research
   441→
   442→### Key Papers
   443→
   444→1. **Optimization of annual planned rail maintenance** (2022)
   445→   - Authors: Oudshoorn et al.
   446→   - Journal: Computer-Aided Civil and Infrastructure Engineering
   447→   - [Link](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/mice.12764)
   448→   - Key finding: Hybrid evolution strategy + greedy outperforms all other approaches
   449→

   451→   - Journal: Transportation Research Part C
   452→   - [Link](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0968090X16302340)
   453→   - Key finding: Weekly problems solvable near-optimally within 1 hour

   480→
   481→### Research Institutions
   482→
   483→- **Zuse Institute Berlin (ZIB)**: 11-year collaboration with Deutsche Bahn
   484→- **Erasmus University Rotterdam**: Railway maintenance optimization
   485→- **TU Delft**: Dutch railway research
   486→- **City University of Hong Kong**: MTR AI scheduling
   487→
   488→---
   489→
   490→## Best Practices
   491→
   492→### 1. Maintenance Trigger Selection
   493→
   494→| Trigger Type | Best For | Limitations |
   495→|--------------|----------|-------------|
   496→| Time-based | Simple assets | Over/under maintenance |
   497→| Mileage-based | Wear-dependent items | Tracking complexity |
   498→| Condition-based | Critical components | Sensor investment |
   499→| Hybrid | Most applications | Implementation complexity |
   500→
   501→From [Railway Technical Website](http://www.railway-technical.com/trains/train-maintenance/):
   502→
   503→> "Rolling stock maintenance can be programmed by mileage, by time, or by condition monitoring. Condition monitoring is most recent and reliable, relying on automated data collection and sensors."
   504→
   505→### 2. Resource Leveling
   506→
   507→**Goal**: Even distribution of workload to avoid peaks and valleys
   508→
   509→**Techniques**:
   510→- Move flexible events within tolerance windows
   511→- Balance by fleet type
   512→- Consider depot-specific constraints
   513→- Account for seasonal variations
   514→
   515→From Tokyo Metro practice:
   516→
   517→> "Allocating an even workload in the annual maintenance plan provides advantages for both resource utilization and service reliability."
   518→
   519→### 3. Mileage Equalization
   520→
   521→**Problem**: Uneven mileage leads to clustered maintenance
   522→
   523→From [ScienceDirect - Long-term Fleet Management](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S2210970623000628):
   524→
   525→> "A sub-optimal choice of wagons can lead to situations requiring complex shunting operations. If wagons were clustered by mileage, the process would be greatly simplified."
   526→
   527→**Solution**: Strategic run assignment to balance fleet mileage
   528→
   529→### 4. Integration with Operations
   530→
   531→**Key Principle**: Maintenance and operations must be coordinated
   532→
   533→From [KONUX and Deutsche Bahn](https://www.konux.com/konux-receives-another-significant-order-from-deutsche-bahn/):
   534→
   535→> "Real-time insights into current and future condition of infrastructure enables scheduling timely interventions such as grinding, welding, or replacement."
   536→
   537→### 5. Performance Metrics
   538→
   539→| Metric | Definition | Target |
   540→|--------|------------|--------|
   541→| MTBF | Mean Time Between Failures | Maximize |
   542→| MDBF | Mean Distance Between Failures | Maximize |
   543→| Availability | % time train is operational | >99% |
   544→| Utilization | % capacity used | 70-90% |
   545→| Compliance | % on-time maintenance | >95% |
   546→
   547→---
   548→
   549→## References
   550→
   551→### Official Documentation & Reports
   552→
   553→1. [JICA Operations and Maintenance of Urban Railways Handbook](https://www.jica.go.jp/english/activities/issues/transport/__icsFiles/afieldfile/2025/03/26/handbook_en_01.pdf)
   554→2. [JICA Rolling Stock Maintenance Manuals (Appendix)](https://openjicareport.jica.go.jp/pdf/12251708_02.pdf)
   555→3. [TfL Asset Management Strategy](https://content.tfl.gov.uk/lu-asset-management-strategy.pdf)
   556→4. [Gurobi Deutsche Bahn Case Study](https://assets.gurobi.com/pdfs/case-study-DB-English-low-res.pdf)
   557→

   559→
   560→5. [RTA ARIIS Launch](https://www.mediaoffice.ae/en/news/2025/june/12-06/rta-rolls-out-the-automated-rail-infrastructure-inspection-system-in-dubai-metro)
   561→6. [SNCF Predictive Maintenance](https://www.sncf.com/en/network-expertise/rolling-stock-division/how-predictive-maintenance-improves-rail-travel)
   562→7. [TfL Predictive Analytics](https://www.computerworld.com/article/1655414/how-tfl-is-using-predictive-analytics-to-keep-the-underground-moving.html)
   563→8. [Deutsche Bahn AI for Maintenance](https://www.bestpractice.ai/ai-case-study-best-practice/deutsche_bahn_reduces_maintenance_cost_by_25%25_and_delay-causing_failures_using_machine_learning)
   564→
   565→### Academic Papers
   566→
   567→9. [Optimization of annual planned rail maintenance (Wiley)](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/mice.12764)
   568→10. [Integrated railway traffic and maintenance planning (ScienceDirect)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0968090X16302340)
   569→11. [DB Hypergraph Optimization (INFORMS)](https://pubsonline.informs.org/doi/10.1287/inte.2020.1069)
   570→12. [MTR Engineering Works Scheduling (AAAI)](https://cdn.aaai.org/AAAI/2005/IAAI05-003.pdf)
   571→13. [Hybrid CP/MIP Framework (ScienceDirect)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0377221717307646)
   572→14. [Operations Research at NS (Springer)](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43069-024-00342-8)
   573→15. [Singapore MRT Scheduling (Springer)](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1023/A:1016002910561)
   574→16. [Depot and Rolling Stock Optimization (Nature)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-57902-5)
   575→
   576→### Technology Vendors
   577→
   578→17. [IBM Maximo](https://www.ibm.com/products/maximo)
   579→18. [Parsons RIMMS Dubai](https://www.parsons.com/project/rail-infrastructure-maintenance-management-system-rimms-dubai-united-arab-emirates-uae/)
   580→19. [KONUX Predictive Maintenance](https://www.konux.com/)
   581→20. [Hitachi ATOS](https://social-innovation.hitachi/en-us/case_studies/atos/)
   582→

   587→23. [TU Delft Transport Planning](https://www.tudelft.nl/en/ceg/about-faculty/departments/transport-planning/)
   588→
   589→---
   590→
   591→## Appendix: Glossary
   592→
   593→| Term | Definition |
   594→|------|------------|
   595→| **CBM** | Condition-Based Maintenance |
